cards.front: What are the three main array types in C++?

cards.text: std::vector is the most {{c1::flexible}} array type, supporting {{c2::dynamic}} resizing and many useful operations.

cards.concept: Best practices for working with std::vector
cards.explanation:
Key guidelines for std::vector:
  • Pass by const reference for read-only
  • Pass by reference to modify
  • Return by value (move semantics)
  • Use signed int for indices (with std::ssize)
  • Prefer range-based for loops when possible
  • Use reserve() when you know final size
  • Avoid std::vector<bool>

cards.back: All elements must have the same type. C++ containers are homogenous.

cards.text: The three main ways to iterate a vector are: {{c1::index-based}} for loops, {{c2::iterator-based}} for loops, and {{c3::range-based}} for loops.

cards.front: What optimization makes returning std::vector by value efficient?
cards.back: Move semantics (and copy elision/RVO). The vector's contents are moved rather than copied, making it fast even for large vectors.

cards.text: A vector's {{c1::length}} is the number of elements in use, while its {{c2::capacity}} is the total storage allocated.

cards.concept: The unsigned length and subscript problem
cards.explanation:
Container sizes return unsigned (std::size_t), but signed is better for indices:
  • Unsigned can't represent negative numbers
  • Wraparound when decrementing from 0
  • Comparison issues with signed values
  • Solution: Use std::ssize() or static_cast to signed

cards.front: How can enumerators improve array indexing?
cards.back: Use enum values as indices for meaningful names. Add a max_count enumerator to automatically track array size. Makes code more readable and maintainable.

cards.text: To use std::vector as a stack: {{c1::push_back()}} to push, {{c2::pop_back()}} to pop, {{c3::back()}} to peek at top.

cards.front: What does it mean for arrays to store elements contiguously?
cards.back: Elements are stored in adjacent memory locations with no gaps. This allows fast, direct access to any element by index.
It's a template specialization that stores bits instead of bool objects, doesn't return real references, and violates container expectations. Use std::vector<char> or std::deque<bool> instead.
